feed.title: Combined summary - [BIP] P2WOTS: 64 Slot Winternitz UTXO's (witness version three)

entry.title: Combined summary - [BIP] P2WOTS: 64 Slot Winternitz UTXO's (witness version three)
entry.summary: The recent discussions within the Bitcoin development community have centered around proposals for new cryptographic signatures to enhance security in anticipation of quantum computing threats. Opus Lux has introduced a proposal titled P2WOTS (Pay-to-Winternitz-One-Time-Signature), which is designed to integrate post-quantum security features into Bitcoin's transaction mechanism using SHA-256 and without incorporating any elliptic curve key materials. The proposed system utilizes a 34-byte scriptPubKey that commits to a Merkle Key Tree with 64 independent WOTS+ one-time key pairs. This method addresses the limitations of WOTS+ by ensuring that each transaction uses a fresh slot key, thus enabling safe address reuse while maintaining the integrity of the one-time signature property. The draft proposal for this can be viewed and further technical feedback from the community is invited at the [BIP draft PR](https://github.com/bitcoin/bips/pull/2194). In response to this proposal, Boris Nagaev suggested considering the SHRINCS signature scheme, which also aims at providing post-quantum security. The SHRINCS model employs an asymmetric tree structure that optimizes the size of signatures by positioning lower index leaves closer to the Merkle root and includes a stateless fallback mechanism to SPHINCS+ in cases where a signer might lose their state information. Additionally, SHRINCS incorporates a constant sum optimization in WOTS to reduce space requirements. More details on the SHRINCS specification and its C++ implementation can be found through these links: [SHRINCS specification](https://github.com/BlockstreamResearch/shrincs-specification), [C++ implementation](https://github.com/BlockstreamResearch/shrincs-cpp/), and a detailed discussion on its features at [DelvingBitcoin.org](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/shrincs-324-byte-stateful-post-quantum-signatures-with-static-backups/2158/). Another noteworthy point of discussion comes from conduition, who critiqued the P2WOTS proposal for several shortcomings. These include the lack of flexibility for gradual migration before quantum threats become prevalent (referred to as Q-day), absence of scripting capabilities which would result in losing many extended Bitcoin features like smart contracts and lightning networks post-Q-day, and various technical oversights such as potential overuse of hash functions leading to unnecessarily large signatures, undefined variables, and critical typos in array-indexing that are security-sensitive. Conduition recommends a thorough review of literature on hash-based cryptography before proceeding with a BIP for it. Opus Lux has also mentioned that a live signet supporting full P2WOTS is available for testing, and a wallet demo can be accessed for interactive experiences via his website, with more information available at [block_opuslux.ar.io](https://block_opuslux.ar.io). He plans to resubmit the BIP once there has been sufficient discussion on these matters within the mailing list. This allows for community-driven refinement of the proposal based on collective expertise and feedback.
